coredata.dc:title: Tailored graphene quantum dots to passivate defects and accelerate charge extraction for all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Quanzhu
Tang, Shouyuan
Yuan, Guohui
Zhu, Wanlu
Huang, Youyuan
Li, Shaojun
Lin, Minjing
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown great potential to simultaneously improve the stability and efficiency of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). However, their preparation is either extremely harsh or inefficient, which severely hampers the extensive research and application. Herein, a novel solid-liquid interfacial exfoliation technique is established to fabricate high-quality GQDs from carbon fiber, in which the throughput of GQDs is up to 20 g in one pot with yield over 90%. To explore their role in PSC, nitrogen and sulfur functionalized GQDs have been further synthesized via a hydrothermal method, and used to regulate the interfacial properties of all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 PSC. Arising from the interaction based on Lewis acid-base chemistry between GQDs and the under-coordinated Pb2+, the detrimental non-radiative recombination is significantly reduced, especially for the functionalized GQDs tailored PSC, leading to the champion efficiency of 9.80% for carbon-electrode based CsPbIBr2 PSCs with excellent long-term stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: All-inorganic perovskite solar cells
Graphene quantum dots
Interface electrochemistry
Defect passivation
Long-term stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation , China ( 2020M672826 ). In addition, I would like to thank my Ph.D. supervisor professor H. Xu and Y. Lu (Ocean University of China) for their spiritual support and life lessons.

The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation, China (2020M672826). In addition, I would like to thank my Ph.D. supervisor professor H. Xu and Y. Lu (Ocean University of China) for their spiritual support and life lessons.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The experimental set-up diagram of the solid-liquid interfacial exfoliation technique to fabricate GQDs. SEM images of (b) raw materials of carbon fiber and (c) carbon fiber after 5 min of electrolysis.

(a) TEM images of GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs. (b) XPS spectra of various GQDs. (c) Structure schematic of GQDs. (d) Optical photographs and (e) PL spectra of various GQDs solutions under 385 nm excitation. (f) and (g) Magnified view of UPS spectra of various GQDs.

Top-view SEM images of CsPbIBr2 films (a) without and (b) with N-GQDs treatment. (c) J-V curves, (d) steady-state power outputs, (e) EQE spectra and (f) PCE distributions of PSCs with and without N-GQDs.

(a) Dark J-V curves of FTO/CsPbIBr2/GQDs/Ag device. (b) PL and (c) TRPL spectra of various perovskite films with and without GQDs treatment. (d) V oc as a function of illuminated light intensity for control and N-GQDs tailored devices.
The long-term stability of control and N-GQDs optimized PSCs under (a) T = 25 °C, RH = 5% and (b) T = 85 °C, RH = 0% conditions without encapsulation.
The photovoltaic parameters of various solar cells characterized under simulated solar light.

head.ce:title: Tailored graphene quantum dots to passivate defects and accelerate charge extraction for all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 perovskite solar cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BTR New Material Group Co., Ltd.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have shown great potential to simultaneously improve the stability and efficiency of perovskite solar cells (PSCs). However, their preparation is either extremely harsh or inefficient, which severely hampers the extensive research and application. Herein, a novel solid-liquid interfacial exfoliation technique is established to fabricate high-quality GQDs from carbon fiber, in which the throughput of GQDs is up to 20 g in one pot with yield over 90%. To explore their role in PSC, nitrogen and sulfur functionalized GQDs have been further synthesized via a hydrothermal method, and used to regulate the interfacial properties of all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 PSC. Arising from the interaction based on Lewis acid-base chemistry between GQDs and the under-coordinated Pb2+, the detrimental non-radiative recombination is significantly reduced, especially for the functionalized GQDs tailored PSC, leading to the champion efficiency of 9.80% for carbon-electrode based CsPbIBr2 PSCs with excellent long-term stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have gradually shown competitive performance to traditional commercial photovoltaics owing to their super-high power conversion efficiency (PCE) up to 25.6% during the last decade [1–3]. As one crucial parameter for practical application, however, their inferior long-term stability under humidity, heat and light irradiation hinders the next-step development [4,5]. Till now, great efforts have been conducted to overcome this dilemma by regulating the film composition, in which the all-inorganic CsPbX3 (X = Cl, I, or Br) perovskites display good tolerance to environment, especially under high temperature condition [6,7]. Unfortunately, there is still a trade-off between efficiency and stability by changing the dosage ratio of I and Br elements, leading to the best phase stability for CsPbBr3 and the highest PCE for CsPbI3 [8,9]. Therefore, with to the aim to balance this issue, all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 perovskite has become one ideal choice for application in tandem solar cells. Comparing to the state-of-the-art PSCs, the PCE loss of CsPbIBr2 PSCs is notorious [10]. As well known, the perovskite film is generally fabricated by a solution-processable spin-coating method, in which the as-prepared film is polycrystalline. Because of the soft crystal lattice of perovskite, which has been widely reported in previous studies [11,12], there are inevitable defects on the surface and grain boundaries even for the single crystal. In this fashion, one major reason to drag the performance improvement is mainly attributed to the enormous surface defects, such as positively charged Pb2+ or negatively charged X- ions, in the perovskite film, which undoubtedly play a role of nonradiative recombination center and lead to serious charge loss [13,14]. Therefore, reducing the defects is regarded as one promising strategy for further performance enhancement.

According to the literature, it is well known that these defects can be healed by doping inorganic metal halides, oxides and organic molecules or polymers [15,16]. Among them, organic materials generally display superiority in passivating defects based on Lewis acid-base chemistry, however, their easy decomposition under elevated temperature undergoes against the concept of all-inorganic PSCs. Furthermore, when employing inorganic metal ions or oxides as passivators, the device performance is highly dependent on the doping amount or the thickness of the insulation layer, increasing the difficulty to achieve the initial purpose. Thus, developing a novel and stable material to passivate the surface defects is important.

Recently, two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterials such as graphene, titanium-carbide MXenes and black phosphorus have shown great potential application in extracting interface charges and passivating defects [17,18]. Owing to the weak interaction between pristine 2D materials and perovskite lattice, the dominate role in enhancing PCE of PSC mainly originates from the construction of physical mode such as van der Waals heterostructure to regulate interface charge transfer pathways [19,20], lowering the in-situ passivation ability to defect of 2D materials. On the contrary, by converting those 2D materials into quantum dots, much more novel properties can be obtained, such as the tunable energy level to accelerate charge extraction and transfer, easy functionalization by changing the terminal groups or atoms. Following this line of thought, comparing to 2D materials, their derived quantum dots can undoubtedly display more advantages to improve the performance of PSC.

To simultaneously realize the multi-functional regulation on perovskite film, herein, a novel solid-liquid interfacial exfoliation technique is first established to realize one-step synthesis of high-quality graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from carbon fiber. Moreover, nitrogen and sulfur functionalized graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs and S-GQDs) have been further synthesized via a hydrothermal method, and used to improve the quality of all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 perovskite film. Comparing to the pristine GQDs, the incorporated N or S atoms can donate electrons to the under-coordinated Pb2+ defects located at perovskite film surface, leading to the improved perovskite film quality. Furthermore, the energy levels of GQDs have been also regulated, which is beneficial for the charge extraction. As a result, a significantly enhanced efficiency of 9.80% has been achieved for hole transporting layer (HTM) free, carbon electrode based CsPbIBr2 PSC, which is much higher than that of control device with 7.43% efficiency. More importantly, the device stability is also increased, which can be attributed to the reduced defect located at the perovskite surface, demonstrating the advantage of this functionalized GQDs as interfacial passivator.

Carbon fiber (CF) was purchased from Toray T700SC of Japan (24 K, 7.0 µm fiber diameter). Ti mesh was purchased from Hebei Yichang Metal Wire Mesh Co. Ltd. Sulfuric acid, ammonium carbonate, potassium 2,5-dihydroxybenzenesulfonate, zinc powder, hydrochloric acid, titanium isopropoxide, titanium tetrachloride, diethanol amine, lead dibromide, cesium iodide and dimethyl sulfoxide were purchased from Sinopharm group. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical exfoliation process was carried out in a two-electrode cell under a constant voltage of 8.0 V, in which carbon fiber bundles (50 g, Toray Industries) were employed as anode and Ti mesh (40 × 40 mm2, TA2) was employed as cathode in 1.0 mol L−1 H2SO4 electrolyte. After 10 h of electrolysis, the electrolyte was filtered through 0.1 µm membrane (nylon membrane), and the resulting electrolyte was dialyzed on a 2000 Da dialysis membrane for 72 h to remove H+ and SO4 2- ions. The brown solution in the dialysis bag was denoted as GQDs.

The prepared GQDs solution (5.0 g L−1, 0.2 L) was mixed with the ammonium carbonate solution (20.0 g L−1, 0.1 L) and then reacted in a hydrothermal autoclave (volume 0.5 L) at 160 °C for 10 h. The resulting dispersion was heated at 90 °C under atmospheric pressure to remove the free carbonate and ammonium. Finally, the obtained brown solution was N-GQDs.

The prepared GQDs solution (5.0 g L−1, 0.2 L) was mixed with the potassium 2,5-dihydroxybenzenesulfonate solution (10.0 g L−1, 0.1 L) and then reacted in a hydrothermal autoclave (volume 0.5 L) at 160 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, the resulting dispersion was dialyzed on a 2000 Da dialysis membrane for 72 h to remove free ions. The brown solution in the dialysis bag was S-GQDs.

The prepared GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs aqueous solution were freeze-dried at −60 °C for 24 h to obtain powders, and then dispersed into the required solvent system with concentration of 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 mg mL−1.

All the processes were conducted in air atmosphere without humidity control. Prior to fabrication of solar cells, an FTO coated glass was etched with zinc powder and HCl (2.0 M) to obtain the desired pattern and then thoroughly rinsed in detergent, acetone, isopropanol, ethanol and deionized water. A compact TiO2 (c-TiO2) layer was deposited onto an FTO glass by spin-coating an ethanol solution of titanium isopropoxide (0.5 M) and diethanol amine (0.5 M) at 7000 rpm for 30 s and annealed in air at 500 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, the c-TiO2 substrate was immersed in an aqueous solution of 0.04 M TiCl4 at 70 °C for 30 min, and then annealed in air for another 30 min at 450 °C.

Prior to transferring the substrates into a glove box, the FTO/c-TiO2 substrates were treated via oxygen plasma cleaning for 3 min. Then, 90 μL of 1.0 M CsPbIBr2 precursor (in which 367 mg PbBr2 and 260 mg CsI were dissolved in 1.0 mL DMSO) was spin-coated onto FTO/c-TiO2 at 1000 rpm for 10 s and 3000 rpm for 50 s, respectively. Particularly, the crystallization process of perovskite thin film included a two-step thermal treatment process. The as prepared thin film was firstly placed on a hotplate at 30 °C for 1 min and then the temperature was increased to 260 °C for 10 min to remove the remnant solvent and further improve the crystallization quality of the perovskite thin film. Subsequently, various concentrations of GQDs isopropanol solution was spin-coated onto the perovskite film surface at 2000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, a carbon back electrode with an active area of 0.09 cm2 was deposited on the perovskite film by a doctor-blade coating method.

The morphology of GQDs and perovskite films were obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI TECNAI G2 F20) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, and field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S4800, Hitachi). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by using a Digital Instrument Nanoscope (Veeco Multimode). The size and zeta potential (ζ) were obtained by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano Zs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Escalab 250Xi) with an Al Kα as the X-ray source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured by using a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectra (UPS) spectra of the samples was characterized by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI PHI5000 VersaProbe III). The time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves were conducted with a Horiba spectrometer excited by 370 nm laser. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) curves of various devices were recorded by IPCE kit developed by Zolix Instruments Co., Ltd. The photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves were measured using a solar simulator (Newport, Oriel Class A, 91195A) under AM 1.5G simulated solar illumination (100 mW cm−2, calibrated by a standard silicon solar cell). A black mask was applied on the surface of cell device to avoid stray light.

Fig. 1(a) shows the schematic diagram to fabricate GQDs by means of a novel solid-liquid interfacial exfoliation technique. In details, one side of the CF bundles was kept continuous contact with electrolyte surface by adjusting the immersion depth of the CF bundles once every 5 min, while another side was connected to the electrode in air condition. Under the constant voltage of 8 V for 10 h, the color of electrolyte was rapidly turned from colorless to golden brown and finally to dark brown along with the reduced diameter from 7.0 µm to 5.7 µm after 5 min treatment (Fig. 1(b and c)), indicating the CF was electrochemically etched. The final yield of GQDs was calculated to be as high as 90%, and the mass of GQDs powder after freeze-drying was up to 20 g. Furthermore, for more demanding applications, N-GQDs and S-GQDs was synthesized via a hydrothermal method.

The mechanism behind this phenomenon can be attributed to three reasons. Firstly, CF has a ribbon-type stacking structure of sp2 carbon (breadth: 5–8 nm, length: 7–14 nm), which can be easily intercalated and stripped off to obtain GQDs [21]. Secondly, the current density of this process is kept uniform during the electrochemical interface reaction, thereby promoting the controllable oxidation and intercalation of O· and SO4 2-. Finally, the mechanical action of bubbles could produce an efficient stripping process, which is conducive to oxidation-cutting into GQDs.

From the TEM images in Fig. 2(a) and the large-scale view images in Fig. S1, it is obvious that both as-prepared GQDs with and without N/S incorporation have an average lateral diameter of dozens of nanometers with thickness of 0.8 nm (see AFM image in Fig. S2), in which the clear lattice fringe of ~2.03 Å (corresponding to (100) crystal plane of GQDs) indicates their good crystallinity with defect-free surface. In details, the average size of the GQDs is gradually increased from 31 nm to 43 nm for N-GQDs and to 50 nm for S-GQDs, as shown in Fig. S3 from dynamic light scattering characterization, an indicator of regulated growth kinetics. After close observation in Fig. 2(a), the irregular edge region with thickness of ~1.0 nm can be found in the three GQDs, mainly attributing to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxy and carbonyl groups, which can be cross-checked by the XPS spectra in Fig. 2(b) (the emergency of N and S signals further confirms the successful doping in the graphene lattice) and FTIR spectra in Fig. S4. To further ensure the incorporation of N and S atoms into GQDs, we have also provided the high-revolution XPS spectra of N 1s and S 2p for N-GQDs and S-GQDs in Fig. S5. Obviously, the strong signals demonstrate the successful incorporation into GQDs. Therefore, the structure of GQDs can be schematically illustrated in Fig. 2c and Fig. S6, providing abundant functional sites to passivate the defects in perovskite film. There is a fact that the optical properties of GQDs are highly dependent on these functional groups [22]. Following this line of thought, the energy level alignment will be undoubtedly regulated. One visible evidence is the change of luminescence photos and PL spectra of three GQDs solutions (Fig. 2(d) and (e)). To better understand the evolution of energy level, we have further characterized the UPS spectra, as shown in Fig. 2(f) and (g), showing the increased work function (WF) upon incorporating N and S atoms according to the WF = E cut-off -21.2 eV [23]. Thus, the valence bands (VB) are determined to be −5.26, −5.14 and −5.05 eV for pristine GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs. Comparing to the −5.36 eV of CsPbIBr2 perovskite [24], the optimized GQDs shows great potential to accelerate charge extraction.

With the aim to better understand the role of GQDs on enhancing efficiency of PSCs, we have subsequently employed GQDs as interfacial regulator to heal the all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 film and fabricated the carbon-electrode based device. In this work, GQDs were spin-coated on the perovskite surface after the annealing process under 260 °C for perovskite formation. In this fashion, upon spin-coating GQDs, the properties of perovskite film are unchanged, especially for the morphology. In addition, because of the small size of GQDs, it is difficult to observe the presence of GQDs by SEM image owing to the technology limit. Therefore, there is nearly no morphology change after GQDs treatment in Fig. 3(a) and (b). Intriguingly, after optimizing the concentration of GQDs solution on perovskite surface, as shown in Fig. S7, the efficiency is significantly enhanced from 7.43% for control device to 8.19%, 9.80% and 9.02% for GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs tailored PSCs, respectively, by recording the characteristic J-V curves under one standard sun irradiation, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The detailed photovoltaic parameters including open-circuit voltage (V oc), short-circuit current density (J sc) and fill factor (FF) are summarized in Table 1. Obviously, all the parameters are increased following the order of N-GQDs > S-GQDs > GQDs > control PSC. To further confirm the efficiency enhancement, IPCE spectra are employed to verify the increased J sc. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the integrated current densities are calculated to be 9.40, 9.61, 10.74 and 10.21 mA cm−2 for control, GQDs, N-GQDs and S-GQDs based PSCs, respectively, which are consistent with results in J-V curves. Together with the steady power outputs (Fig. 3(e)) recorded under the bias of maximal power points, we can conclude that the tailored GQDs can effectively improve the charge scavenging. It should be noted that there is a slight difference between the steady PCE in Fig. 3(e) and the J-V curves in Fig. 3(c), which is mainly attributed to the presence of hysteresis effect in PSC device. To check the reproducibility of PSCs with GQDs, we have fabricated 15 devices for four batches (Fig. 3(f)). The statistics of the PCE distribution confirms the good reproducibility of our results.

In order to get deep understanding on the origin of enhanced PCE, we have then qualitatively estimated the defect density of CsPbIBr2 films with and without GQDs treatment through space charge limited current (SCLC) methods. As shown in Fig. 4(a), by recording the dark J-V curves of electron-only devices, the defect density (N t) is calculated by the following equation [25]:
V TFL = (eN t L 2)/(2εε 0)

in which the V TFL represents the trap-filled limit voltage, e is the elementary charge of the electron, L is the thickness of perovskite film, ε is relative dielectric constant, and ε 0 is vacuum permittivity. Thus, comparing to the control perovskite film with V TFL of 1.50 V, the reduced V TFL values of 1.42 V, 1.32 V and 1.37 V for GQDs, N-GQDs, S-GQDs based films demonstrate that the surface defects in CsPbIBr2 film are reduced, which can be attributed to the interaction between O-, S- or N-containing functional groups in GQDs and under- coordinated Pb2+. It should be noted that the incorporation of S and N atoms into the graphene surface can provide more superficial sites to passivate defects (the negative charged feature determined by the Zeta potential characterization in Fig. S8), which is beneficial to suppress the detrimental non-radiative recombination. To understand the charge extraction ability of GQDs in this work, we have characterized the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra and time-resolved PL (TRPL) decay spectra, the results are shown in Fig. 4(b) and (c). Upon spin-coating GQDs onto the surface of perovskite film, the photoluminescence intensity of the perovskite film is significantly reduced. This is reverse to the increased PL intensity along with reduced defects [26,27]. Taking the energy level alignment of GQDs into consideration, the mechanism behind this phenomenon can be attributed to the accelerated charge extraction at the CsPbIBr2/carbon interface. This is echoed by the significantly reduced average carrier lifetime from 2.56 ns to 1.25 ns after treatment with N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 4(c). Arising from the dual functions: defect passivation and charge extraction, the unwanted non-radiative recombination is expected to be minimum when assembling the healed perovskite film into solar cell. From the relationship between V oc and light intensity (I), a reduced ideality factor from 1.60k B T/e for control device to 1.36k B T/e is obtained according to the equation [28]: V oc = nk B Tln(I)/e + constant, where n is the ideal factor, k B is the Boltzmann constant, and T is the temperature, as shown in Fig. 4(d), demonstrating the reduced non-radiative recombination within a PSC. All the characterizations show the positive effect of GQDs on the performance improvement.

As well known, apart from solar-to-electric conversion efficiency, the long-term stability is another important criterion to evaluate the application of PSC in practical environment [29–31]. As shown in Fig. 5(a), we have tracked the efficiency of control and N-GQDs optimized PSCs, which are stored in air condition with relative humidity (RH) of 5% and temperature (T) of 25 °C without encapsulation. After 60 days storage, the control device can only remain 85% of initial efficiency, while the GQDs tailored PSC still sustain 90% of initial value. Furthermore, after treatment under 85 °C, as shown in Fig. 5(b), the optimized PSC also displays improved stability. The results demonstrate that the GQDs have dual function to simultaneously increase the efficiency and tolerance to environment.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized high-quality GQDs by a novel solid-liquid interfacial exfoliation technique with highly efficient (20 g every 10 h) and high-yield (90%) from carbon fiber. Moreover, the N-/S- incorporated GQDs as an interfacial mediator can passivate the defective surface and accelerate the charge extraction in all-inorganic CsPbIBr2 PSC. Owing to the interaction between GQDs and perovskite, an enhanced efficiency of 9.80% has been obtained, which is much higher than that of control device. Considering the diversity in regulating physical and chemical properties of GQDs, there may be more room to improve the performance of PSC.

Quanzhu Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shouyuan Tang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Investigation. Guohui Yuan: Validation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Wanlu Zhu: Resources, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Youyuan Huang: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Shaojun Li: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Minjing Lin: Resources, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledged financial support provided by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation, China (2020M672826). In addition, I would like to thank my Ph.D. supervisor professor H. Xu and Y. Lu (Ocean University of China) for their spiritual support and life lessons.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.162529.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Organometal halide perovskites as visible-light sensitizers for photovoltaic cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kojima, K. Teshima, Y. Shirai, T. Miyasaka, Organometal halide perovskites as visible-light sensitizers for photovoltaic cells, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131 (2009) 6050–6051.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoscale localized contacts for high fill factors in polymer-passivated perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Peng, D. Walter, Y. Ren, M. Tebyetekerwa, Y. Wu, T. Duong, Q. Lin, J. Li, T. Lu, M.A. Mahmud, O.L. C. Lem, S. Zhao, W. Liu, Y. Liu, H. Shen, L. Li, F. Kremer, H.T. Nguyen, D.-Y. Choi, K.J. Weber, K.R. Catchpole, T.P. White, Nanoscale localized contacts for high fill factors in polymer-passivated perovskite solar cells, Science 371 (2021) 390–395.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pseudo-halide anion engineering for α-FAPbI3 perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Jeong, M. Kim, J. Seo, H. Lu, P. Ahlawat, A. Mishra, Y. Yang, M.A. Hope, F.T. Eickemeyer, M. Kim, Y.J. Yoon, I.W. Choi, B.P. Darwich, S.J. Choi, Y. Jo, J.H. Lee, B. Walker, S.M. Zakeeruddin, L. Emsley, U. Rothlisberger, A. Hagfeldt, D.S. Kim, M. Grätzel, J.Y. Kim, Pseudo-halide anion engineering for α-FAPbI3 perovskite solar cells, Nature 592 (2021) 381–385.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diffusion engineering of ions and charge carriers for stable efficient perovskite solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Bi, H. Chen, F. Xie, Y. Wu, W. Chen, Y. Su, A. Islam, M. Grätzel, X. Yang, L. Han, Diffusion engineering of ions and charge carriers for stable efficient perovskite solar cells, Nat. Commun. 8 (2017) 15330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stability of perovskite solar cells: a prospective on the substitution of the a cation and X anion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, Z. Shi, T. Li, Y. Chen, W. Huang, Stability of perovskite solar cells: A prospective on the substitution of the a cation and X anion, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 56 (2017) 1190–1212.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inorganic perovskite solar cells: an emerging member in photovoltaic community

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Duan, H. Xu, W.E. I. Sha, Y. Zhao, Y. Wang, X. Yang, Q. Tang, Inorganic perovskite solar cells: An emerging member in photovoltaic community, J. Mater. Chem. A 7 (2019) 21036–21068.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Untapped potentials of inorganic metal halide perovskite solar cells

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-purity inorganic perovskite films for solar cells with 9.72% efficiency
